metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the GitHub reconcile bridge workflow (and local clone paths) explicitly fetch `refs/reconciler/*` before any lock acquire/release so that `--force-with-lease=refs/reconciler/lock:<old>` has a valid remote-tracking ref to compare against; add the required refspec fetch to the workflow if absent.
findings.finding: The plan specifies using `--force-with-lease=<ref>:<old>` for the over-the-wire push of refs/reconciler/* to origin, but the GitHub reconcile bridge workflow's fetch step (checkout@v4 with fetch-depth: 1) does not fetch refs/reconciler/* — meaning the local clone will have no tracking ref for refs/reconciler/lock, and `--force-with-lease=refs/reconciler/lock:<old>` will fail with 'stale info' or be rejected because the lease cannot be verified against an absent remote-tracking ref.
findings.location: Design section: 'Over the wire use explicit --force-with-lease=<ref>:<old> — never the bare lease form.' / .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml fetch step
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit `git fetch origin refs/reconciler/*:refs/reconciler/*` step (or expand the checkout refspec) in the reconcile-bridge workflow before the reconciler runs, and document this as a required companion change to the ref-lock module.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document that a `refs/reconciler/*` ref pointing directly to a blob SHA (not a commit) propagates correctly across `git fetch`/`git push` to GitHub and is GC-safe; consider using a lightweight commit (tree+blob) as the ref target instead if blob-SHA refs are unreliable.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a git ref whose VALUE is a blob SHA (ref → blob, not ref → commit), but does not verify that this non-canonical ref type reliably propagates across `git fetch`/`git push` to GitHub and is protected from GC. Standard git fetch pack-protocol advertises refs as commits; a ref pointing directly to a blob SHA sits outside the normal reachability graph and may not transfer correctly across all server implementations or be protected by GitHub's GC policies.
findings.location: Design section: 'Move the pass-lock and phase-gate OFF the working tree into a bare git ref: refs/reconciler/lock (and gate), ref -> blob.'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a minimal git commit (empty tree + blob as a file, e.g. `git commit-tree`) as the ref target instead of a raw blob SHA. This is the pattern used by git-notes (refs/notes/*) and is known to be fetch/push/GC-safe. The CAS mechanics (update-ref <new_commit_sha> <old_commit_sha>) are identical.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that GitHub (with GITHUB_TOKEN + contents:write) accepts push and fetch of refs/reconciler/* on a hosted repo, and update reconcile-bridge.yml with explicit fetch/push refspecs for refs/reconciler/lock before committing to the ref-lock design (spike or docs citation required).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that refs/reconciler/* can be pushed to and fetched from the GitHub remote using --force-with-lease=<ref>:<old>, but GitHub's hosted-repo ref permissions and the existing reconcile-bridge workflow refspecs do not cover this namespace — the workflow only fetches '+tickets:refs/remotes/origin/tickets' and pushes HEAD:tickets. Whether GitHub accepts GITHUB_TOKEN-authenticated pushes to refs/reconciler/* on a hosted repo is unverified and is a blocking external capability gap.
findings.location: Design section: 'Over the wire use explicit --force-with-lease=<ref>:<old>'; .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml:102 (fetch step) and :242 (push step)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a spike step: run `git push origin refs/reconciler/lock-test` and `git fetch origin refs/reconciler/lock-test` from a GHA job with contents:write, confirm exit 0. If confirmed, add explicit refspecs to the fetch and push steps in reconcile-bridge.yml (e.g., `git fetch origin '+refs/reconciler/*:refs/reconciler/*'` and push via `git push origin refs/reconciler/lock:<sha> --force-with-lease=refs/reconciler/lock:<old>`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a criterion (or extend AC#2) that explicitly specifies the phase-gate blob format and its self-healing semantics (same lease/heartbeat/CAS-break contract as the lock, or a documented rationale for a simpler/different structure).
findings.finding: The phase-gate blob format and self-healing behavior are never specified anywhere in the plan body, yet the phase-gate is a named deliverable in AC#1. AC#2 specifies the lock blob (holder + lease duration + HLC heartbeat + fence counter) but only says 'Lock blob carries...' — the phase-gate blob content, expiry semantics, and whether it requires the same heartbeat/CAS-break mechanism are left unaddressed. This is an uncovered deliverable: the migration of the phase-gate lacks a criterion governing its structure or self-healing behavior.
findings.location: AC#1 (phase-gate); AC#2 (lock blob only); Design section ('Move the pass-lock and phase-gate OFF the working tree')
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC#2: 'Lock blob and phase-gate blob each carry holder + relative lease duration + HLC heartbeat + monotonic fence counter; the holder renews at ~lease/3. If the phase-gate requires no expiry semantics, document that rationale explicitly here.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the heartbeat renewal execution model (background thread vs. pipeline-checkpoint renewal vs. separate process) and address concurrent git subprocess safety — confirm whether git ref operations in a background thread are safe alongside the main thread's git calls, or bound renewals to explicit quiescent checkpoints in the reconcile pipeline.
findings.finding: The heartbeat renewal mechanism is not specified for the actual execution model: the reconciler is a single-threaded Python process that may run 10-60 minutes (GHA timeout-minutes: 60). Renewing `refs/reconciler/lock` at ~lease/3 requires either a background thread making concurrent `git push` calls while the main thread is also issuing git subprocesses, or a separate process. Neither is addressed. Concurrent git subprocess calls into the same repo without coordination can hit git's own internal ref-lock (`.git/packed-refs.lock`, `.git/refs/reconciler/lock.lock`), and a background thread blocking on a push (network) while the main thread holds no Python lock around subprocess calls is a real deadlock/contention surface.
findings.location: Design section — 'Self-heal: the holder renews the lease by heartbeat at ~lease/3'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the heartbeat renewal execution model (e.g. daemon thread, asyncio task, or periodic subprocess) and address how it interacts with the reconciler's synchronous try/finally flow, GIL contention with subprocess-heavy pass execution, and cleanup on abnormal exit.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the holder renews the lock lease via heartbeat at '~lease/3' but does not specify the heartbeat execution model — whether the heartbeat runs on a background thread, a subprocess, or some other mechanism within the reconciler process. The current reconciler is a synchronous Python process with no background thread infrastructure. Introducing a concurrent heartbeat thread is a non-trivial architectural addition that interacts with the reconciler's existing try/finally lock release pattern and its single-process assumption.
findings.location: AC#2 / Design: 'the holder renews the lease by heartbeat at ~lease/3'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the heartbeat renewal mechanism concretely in the plan (e.g., daemon thread, `threading.Timer`, cooperative `yield` points at pass stage boundaries, or a separate subprocess) and address why the project's prior heartbeat-lease rejection (snapshot/janitor.py) does not apply here.
findings.finding: The plan's self-heal design requires the lock holder to renew the lease by heartbeat at ~lease/3 ('the holder renews the lease by heartbeat at ~lease/3'), but the reconciler is a synchronous single-pass process with no existing background thread or async infrastructure — this is a novel concurrency primitive the codebase has never used, and the plan does not specify the implementation approach (thread, subprocess, signal-based timer, or cooperative yield points).
findings.location: Design section: 'Self-heal: the holder renews the lease by heartbeat at ~lease/3'; AC line: 'Lock blob carries holder + relative lease duration + HLC heartbeat + monotonic fence counter; the holder renews at ~lease/3'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the lock blob wire format (encoding, field names, units) co-located with AC#2, e.g. a JSON schema or a named newline-delimited structure, so acquire/heartbeat/steal/expiry-check all share one canonical serializer.
findings.finding: The lock blob wire format is referenced in AC#2 with four named fields (holder id, relative lease duration, HLC heartbeat, monotonic fence counter) but the serialization schema is never defined anywhere in the plan. An executor implementing the new ref-lock module cannot write a parser or serializer without knowing the encoding (newline-delimited text? JSON? struct?). The existing implementation uses a 2-field newline-delimited format; the new 4-field format with a 'relative' duration is structurally different and uncomposed.
findings.location: Design section ('Lock blob carries…') and AC#2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Lock blob format' subsection to Design: e.g. JSON with keys `holder_id` (str), `lease_duration_ns` (int, relative), `heartbeat_hlc` (int, HLC tick), `fence` (int, monotonic counter). Specify that all readers/writers use this schema; the expiry check is `heartbeat_hlc + lease_duration_ns < now_hlc`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the phase-gate ref (`refs/reconciler/gate`) blob format and its access protocol (operator-written vs. CAS-managed, blob content, migration path from the old file) as a named sub-section of Design or a dedicated AC.
findings.finding: The phase-gate blob format for `refs/reconciler/gate` is never specified. AC#1 requires both pass-lock and phase-gate to move to `refs/reconciler/*`, but while AC#2 specifies the lock blob schema (incompletely), the gate ref's blob content is completely unaddressed. Today `_GATE_FILE` contains a bare mode string (e.g. 'bootstrap-strict'); whether the gate ref blob retains this format, adds versioning, or acquires self-healing fields is unspecified.
findings.location: AC#1 ('Pass-lock and phase-gate live in refs/reconciler/*') and Design section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the expiry computation formula and the fence counter increment rule with the blob schema definition, specifying the clock source (HLC), the expiry predicate, and exactly when the counter is incremented (e.g., on each successful acquire, not on heartbeat renewals).
findings.finding: The expiry-check decision rule ('a contender CAS-breaks only an expired lock') is stated as a behavior but is not co-located with the blob schema that makes it computable. The plan never specifies the formula for determining expiry (e.g., `heartbeat_hlc + lease_duration_ns < now`) or the clock source to use (HLC tick vs. wall time). Separately, the monotonic fence counter's increment placement is unspecified: it is listed as a blob field but the plan never says who increments it (acquirer? each heartbeat renewal? the breaker on steal?).
findings.location: Design section ('Self-heal: the holder renews… a contender may CAS-break only an expired lock') and AC#3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or expand-contract sequencing) for the three destructive steps — retry band-aid removal, gitattributes entry removal, and file-based lock retirement — e.g., a feature flag or a defined commit-revert procedure, so the team can recover cleanly if the ref-lock has a production defect.
findings.finding: The plan removes the `b859` retry band-aid, the `.reconciler-*` gitattributes entry, and the file-based lock path without providing an explicit rollback or back-out plan. If the new ref-lock scheme has a production defect (e.g., force-with-lease CAS failures under concurrent multi-clone push pressure, or heartbeat renewal bugs), there is no documented path to revert to the prior working state. T4 requires that the REMEDY be an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — acknowledgement of the breaking change alone is insufficient.
findings.location: Design section ('remove the b859-8fa1 retry band-aid', 'remove .reconciler-* from merge=ours') and AC lines 4–5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Rollback path' subsection to the Design: (1) keep the retry band-aid behind a `RECONCILER_REF_LOCK=1` env/flag for one release cycle so it can be toggled off if needed; (2) define a named git tag or branch point before the destructive removals land so `git revert <range>` is a documented, tested option; (3) state the explicit go/no-go signal (e.g., 'N full reconcile cycles observed with zero wedge events') that triggers final removal of the fallback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or expand an existing one) requiring a new ADR / CLAUDE.md update capturing the ref-lock architecture: why bare-git-ref over file, the HLC+fence-counter blob format, the ~lease/3 renewal ratio rationale, and the CAS-break-on-expiry steal protocol.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision (moving coordination state from working-tree files to bare git refs with HLC-heartbeat leases and a fence counter) but does not specify that a new ADR will be written to capture this decision; prior ADRs 0026-0029 are referenced but cover the separate binding-data workstream, not the ref-lock coordination pattern.
findings.location: Design section / Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'An ADR is written documenting the refs/reconciler/* lock design (ref-as-lock rationale, blob format, lease/heartbeat parameters, steal protocol) and referenced from CLAUDE.md.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the acquire atomicity contract for cross-clone coordination: specify whether the atomic acquire unit is `git push --force-with-lease=refs/reconciler/lock:<expected_sha>` (remote CAS, the correct cross-clone primitive) or a local update-ref followed by a push, and explain how check_pass_lock fetches the remote ref before reading to avoid stale-local-ref false negatives.
findings.finding: The acquire CAS mechanism conflates a local `git update-ref` with a remote push, leaving the cross-clone atomicity sequence unspecified. The plan states the 'authoritative ref lives on origin' and specifies `--force-with-lease=<ref>:<old>` for the over-the-wire operation, but the existing CAS primitive (`git update-ref refs/heads/tickets <new> <old>` in `_advisory_lock.py:575`) is a LOCAL ref operation. For `refs/reconciler/lock` to coordinate across clones, acquire must: (a) fetch the remote ref to get the current SHA, (b) CAS locally, then (c) push — but steps (a)-(c) are not atomic; a competing clone can acquire between (a) and (c). A push with `--force-with-lease=refs/reconciler/lock:<old_sha>` IS the correct cross-clone atomic operation, but it is not how the 'existing ref-CAS primitive' works. Similarly, `check_pass_lock` currently reads a local tree file; checking a remote ref requires a fetch first (network call on every lock check).
findings.location: Design section — 'Create-only CAS acquire and CAS release over the ref, reusing the existing ref-CAS primitive already in _advisory_lock.py:92-111'
findings.suggested_fix: The correct cross-clone acquire is: `git push origin <new_blob_sha>:refs/reconciler/lock --force-with-lease=refs/reconciler/lock:<expected_sha>` — this is the atomic remote CAS. The existing local `update-ref` primitive can then be dropped or relegated to a post-push local mirror. Document this explicitly so the implementer does not accidentally build a local-only lock.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the heartbeat renewal failure fallback: what the holder does when a renewal CAS fails (e.g., log a warning and continue the pass, treating the remaining pass as opportunistic, given the idempotency guarantee) vs. when to treat it as fatal.
findings.finding: The heartbeat renewal sub-step ('holder renews the lease at ~lease/3') has no specified failure fallback. If the CAS push of a heartbeat renewal fails — because a contender already broke the lock and replaced the ref, or because of a transient git/network error — the plan is silent on what the holder should do: abort the pass, proceed lock-free, retry renewal, or log-and-continue. This is a named sub-step with an actionable outcome path that is completely unspecified.
findings.location: Design section ('Self-heal: the holder renews the lease by heartbeat at ~lease/3') and AC#2
findings.suggested_fix: Given the plan's own framing ('efficiency lock, not a correctness lock'), the natural fallback is: log a WARN, emit a metric/event, and continue the pass to completion — a stolen lock degrades to two concurrent idempotent passes, which the design already accepts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document an explicit rollback procedure in the plan: either a tested revert commit strategy, a flag to re-enable the file-lock fallback, or a manual recovery runbook (e.g., 'delete refs/reconciler/lock via git push origin :refs/reconciler/lock to force-release a wedged lock') that operators can execute without a code deploy.
findings.finding: The plan removes the b859 retry band-aid and the .reconciler-* .gitattributes entry but specifies no explicit rollback procedure if the ref-lock implementation misbehaves in production; once the old fallbacks are deleted, quick reversion requires a non-trivial code revert.
findings.location: AC: 'The b859-8fa1 lock-retry band-aid (_advisory_lock.py:73-89, 263-291) is removed once the self-healing lock supersedes it.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A manual recovery runbook is documented (e.g., in CLAUDE.md or ops docs): force-deleting refs/reconciler/lock via git push to break a wedged lock without a code deploy; this runbook is tested in CI by simulating a crashed holder.'}

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the default lease duration (e.g. 120 s) and the minimum/maximum bounds an operator may configure, so that the 'never wedges beyond one lease interval' success criterion is concretely measurable.
findings.finding: The lease duration (the scalar value stored in the blob and used to determine expiry) is never specified — the executor must 'choose an appropriate X' without guidance.
findings.location: Design section — 'Lock blob carries: holder id + a relative lease duration...' and AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Design section: 'Default lease duration: 120 s (configurable via `RECONCILER_LOCK_LEASE_SECONDS`); minimum 30 s, maximum 600 s. Heartbeat fires at lease/3 (≈40 s default). A lock is expired when `now > acquired_at + lease_duration`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete lease duration (default value and/or configurable range) in the Design section so that the Success Criterion 'never wedges beyond one lease interval' is operationally observable and testable.
findings.finding: The lease duration is never specified anywhere in the plan (not in Design, not in ACs, not in Success Criteria). The Success Criterion 'a crashed pass-holder never wedges the reconciler beyond one lease interval' is only partially observable without knowing what that interval is or how it will be configured.
findings.location: Design section ('relative lease duration') and Success Criteria ('beyond one lease interval')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Design: 'Default lease duration: 5 minutes (configurable via RECONCILER_LOCK_LEASE_SECONDS); renewal fires at lease/3 (~100 s); a contender may break after lease elapses from the last heartbeat timestamp in the blob.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC6's idempotency assertion: either cite existing tests that cover it (e.g. `tests/unit/reconciler/test_idempotency.py`) or require a new regression test analogous to AC3/AC4.
findings.finding: AC6 says idempotency is 'preserved/asserted' but does not specify how — whether by a new test, an existing test, a code-level `assert`, or a design argument — leaving the executor to decide the assertion mechanism.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 6: 'Reconcile-pass idempotency preserved/asserted (the invariant that makes an efficiency lock safe)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC#6's 'asserted' language by naming the concrete verification mechanism (e.g., an existing integration test suite that exercises double-pass, a new unit test for idempotency, or an explicit note that 'existing green suite is sufficient').
findings.finding: AC#6 says 'Reconcile-pass idempotency preserved/asserted' but no plan-body section describes what mechanism, test, or check constitutes the assertion. The plan treats idempotency as a pre-existing invariant ('Reconcile passes are idempotent') but no deliverable in the Design section or elsewhere describes a new test or runtime guard that 'asserts' it. The verify step of AC#6 is therefore unanchored — a reviewer cannot determine what to check to call this criterion satisfied.
findings.location: AC#6; Context section ('Framed honestly as an EFFICIENCY lock')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'preserved/asserted' with either 'preserved (existing suite sufficient — no new test required)' or add a sub-bullet naming the specific test/file that asserts double-pass idempotency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the lease duration and heartbeat interval (~lease/3) are configurable (e.g., via environment variable or reconciler config), not hardcoded, and add this to an AC.
findings.finding: The lease duration and ~lease/3 heartbeat renewal cadence are referenced as fixed design choices without being specified as configurable parameters, yet these thresholds are highly likely to need tuning as the reconciler cadence changes (especially with RECONCILE_CONTINUOUS active).
findings.location: Design section: 'the holder renews the lease by heartbeat at ~lease/3'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Lease duration and heartbeat renewal interval are configurable via environment variable (e.g., RECONCILER_LOCK_LEASE_SECONDS, RECONCILER_LOCK_HEARTBEAT_RATIO) with documented defaults.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollout strategy specifying either: (a) a feature flag (e.g., RECONCILER_REF_LOCK=1) that enables the new ref-lock path while keeping the file-lock fallback, enabling a staged cutover across all actors; or (b) a documented atomic deploy ordering ensuring all actors (local + GA bridge) switch simultaneously with explicit coexistence behavior during the transition window.
findings.finding: The plan performs a hard cutover from file-based locks to ref-based locks with no staged rollout or feature flag: two concurrent actors (local clones and the GitHub Actions reconcile bridge) must simultaneously adopt the new locking mechanism, but no coexistence or deploy-ordering strategy is specified.
findings.location: Design section / Acceptance Criteria (all ACs assume complete replacement)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A RECONCILER_REF_LOCK feature flag gates the new ref-lock path; the old file-lock path remains functional until the flag is enabled on all actors, after which the band-aid and .gitattributes entry are removed in a follow-up.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or explicit test specification for the heartbeat renewal path: verify that an active holder that renews at ~lease/3 does NOT have its lock CAS-broken by a contender (i.e., test the non-expiry case, not just the expiry case).
findings.finding: The heartbeat renewal path — 'holder renews the lease at ~lease/3' — is stated in Design and referenced in the lock-blob AC, but no regression test AC covers it. A renewal failure (e.g., renewal silently no-ops or renews with wrong clock semantics) could defeat the self-healing goal without tripping any specified test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 2 ('Lock blob carries ... holder renews at ~lease/3') and AC 3 ('Stale-lock self-heal ... regression test')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC 3: 'and a contender does NOT break a still-valid (non-expired) lock held by an active renewer (regression test for the renewal path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'HLC heartbeat' in the lock blob means a true Hybrid Logical Clock (requiring a library or new implementation) or a plain wall-clock timestamp used as a heartbeat marker; if the former, name the library and add it to scope; if the latter, rename the field and note the clock-skew implications for lease expiry.
findings.finding: The lock blob is specified to carry an 'HLC heartbeat' field, but no HLC (Hybrid Logical Clock) implementation exists in the codebase and no library is named. The oss-comparison doc (docs/oss-comparison-and-remediation.md, Phase 2, G1) explicitly places HLC as a *future planned addition* to the event-ordering system, not something currently available. Using 'HLC heartbeat' in the lock blob without a concrete implementation plan or library choice is an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: AC#2: 'Lock blob carries holder + relative lease duration + HLC heartbeat + monotonic fence counter; the holder renews at ~lease/3.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit cutover/cleanup step: (a) a one-shot migration commit that deletes any existing `.reconciler-*` tree files from the tickets branch before retiring the old code path, and (b) an idempotent UPDATE path in `_commit_gitattributes` (not just create-if-absent) that removes the `.reconciler-* merge=ours` line from existing `.gitattributes` files on re-init, mirroring the `_commit_gitignore` migration pattern at `init.py:366-383`.
findings.finding: The plan has no cleanup step for pre-existing `.reconciler-*` tree files that may reside on the tickets branch at cutover, and no specified rollback sequence for the `.gitattributes` commit (a distributed git artifact). AC#4 only prevents future commits of `.reconciler-*` files but leaves any currently-committed stale lock/gate file orphaned — old-code clones that have not yet upgraded will still interpret that stale tree file as a held lock, wedging them until they upgrade or the file is manually removed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC#4; Design section 'remove .reconciler-* from the merge=ours .gitattributes'; _commands/init.py:55-56 (_GITATTRIBUTES / _commit_gitattributes)
findings.suggested_fix: Model the .gitattributes update on the existing _commit_gitignore migration (init.py:366-383): read the current committed .gitattributes, strip the '.reconciler-* merge=ours' line if present, and commit the result — making _commit_gitattributes idempotent for both addition and removal. Similarly, add an init-time migration step (or a dedicated migration function called from init_core) that runs `git rm --cached .reconciler-pass-lock .reconciler-phase-gate` on the tickets branch if those files exist, so no stale tree-file lock survives cutover.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit 'Depends on: 524d-00fa-66ef-4432, 7522-129b-1380-418c' prerequisite to child 2711's description/AC so the ordering constraint is machine-checkable.
findings.finding: Child 2711 relies on `renew()` (heartbeat at ~lease/3) introduced in child 7522, but 2711 does not declare 7522 as an explicit prerequisite by ticket ID — only the informal 'C1+C2' shorthand appears in the prose description, not in the AC list or a Depends-On field.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Depends on: 524d-00fa-66ef-4432 (C1), 7522-129b-1380-418c (C2)' line to child 2711's header or AC block, and reference 7522 by ticket ID wherever renew() / lease-expiry-steal are assumed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit 'Depends on: 2711-21bf-ab71-4136 (C3)' prerequisite to child 9ed2's description so it cannot be landed before the ref-lock wiring is complete.
findings.finding: Child 9ed2 does not explicitly declare that it must be landed after 2711; if 9ed2 is applied before 2711 (removing the b859 retry band-aid before the self-healing ref-lock is wired into the reconciler), the reconciler would have no contention-resolution mechanism at all during the gap.
findings.location: child 9ed2-e0a1-c69c-44d4
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'Depends on: 2711-21bf-ab71-4136 (C3)' to 9ed2's header, mirroring the explicit dependency language used in 7522 ('Builds directly on the C1 primitive').

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
